mappings.rationale: Directly covers the history of the Nilotic Sudan and Ethiopia, including medieval Christian kingdoms, Islamic expansion, and Egyptian occupation.
The medieval Christian kingdoms of Nobatia, Maqurrah, and Alwah, and the Coptic Church's influence on Ethiopian culture, are central to this section.
The spread of Muslim domination from c. 639, Mamluk attacks, and the rise of Islamic governance in the Sudan are key themes.
The Ethiopian Orthodox Church (Coptic tradition) and the Christian kingdoms of the Nilotic Sudan are connected to the broader history of Eastern Christianity.
The Egyptian occupation of Sudan under Muhammad Ali, and the Aksumite empire's decline, illustrate patterns of imperial expansion and collapse.
The interactions between indigenous beliefs, Christianity, and Islam in the Nilotic Sudan and Ethiopia are part of the broader landscape of African religions.
The rise of the Funj kingdom and the spread of Islam in the Sudan after c. 1500 represent a significant chapter in Islam's global expansion.
The Portuguese contacts with Ethiopia in the 16th century were partly motivated by crusading ideology and the search for Prester John.
The brief conciliation with the Roman Catholic Church in early 17th-century Ethiopia represents an important episode of Catholic missionary activity.
